### Post by drowner on 2008-10-07
It looks like your fstab has it called 'sde', but it doesn't appear there is an sde!

Can you try:
```

sudo fdisk -l
```

That's a little L, NOT the number 1, by the way

Edit: are these drives always connected to your computer?

### Post by drowner on 2008-10-07
Hold up - I see a typo.

The line for you simple drive in your fstab should be:

```
/dev/sde1 /media/SimpleDrive ntfs-3g defaults, locale=en_US.UTF-8 0 0 

```

Are you comfortable with backing up, editing and saving the fstab, or would you like me to go through that with you?

### Post by 3rdalbum on 2008-10-07
Why are you putting your external hard drive into the fstab? That's really only for drives that will *always* be available at bootup that *need* to be mounted early on in the startup process.

Easier suggestion for you. Your external hard disk is /dev/sde1, from the looks of things. So take out all lines from your fstab about /dev/sde1, and you can do this:

```
pmount -t ntfs-3g /dev/sde1 simpledrive
```

The hard disk will appear under /media/simpledrive.

To unmount:

```
pumount simpledrive
```

---

### Post by 3rdalbum on 2008-10-07
> **forensicater said:**
> Where Do I insert your code into? As a new line?

Just into a terminal window, not into the /etc/fstab file.

> What happens if I do otherwise and leave it listed as a drive that will always be there?

If you turn on the drive or plug it in after the machine starts up, it won't be mounted. It will appear at startup though.

It's just that it's easier for you to "pmount" it than fiddle with the fstab.

If you "pmount" while it's in the fstab, then I don't think the pmount step will work. Even if the fstab line is wrong, pmount will still fail. So it's important that if you decide to use pmount, that you also remove any fstab entries pertaining to the drive.

> I just want to not have to do this everytime I shutdown my laptop.

You can set it up as a launcher so all you need to do is double-click a desktop icon and it will mount the drive.

---

### Post by drowner on 2008-10-07
3rdalbum is, as usual, correct.

Out of interest, 3rdalbum, if he has more than one external drive plugged into his computer at start up, is it possible that sde1 could be assigned to a DIFFERENT external drive, and therefore causing a problem with mounting, or mounting the wrong drive in the wrong place?

Are ntfs drives not automounted?

---

### Post by 3rdalbum on 2008-10-07
Right-click the desktop, select "Create Launcher..." change the type to "Application in Terminal", type in a name, and put the pmount command that I mentioned before in the "command" field.

---

### Post by 3rdalbum on 2008-10-07
> **drowner said:**
> Out of interest, 3rdalbum, if he has more than one external drive plugged into his computer at start up, is it possible that sde1 could be assigned to a DIFFERENT external drive, and therefore causing a problem with mounting, or mounting the wrong drive in the wrong place?

It's possible. Actually I just found that I was wrong about something: /dev/sde1 can be in the fstab and pmount will still work. 

The solution to the potential problem is to use UUIDs instead of device file names; but I don't know too much about UUIDs.

> Are ntfs drives not automounted?

They should do, but maybe not as read/write. The reason why I know about pmount is because my Walkman doesn't automount on Hardy anymore; maybe the same bug is stopping the original poster's hard disk from mounting automatically.

### Post by drowner on 2008-10-07

### Post by 3rdalbum on 2008-10-07
> **forensicater said:**
> I just did the launcher and I get an error about the log file indicating an unclean shut down. Should I restart my laptop despite me knowing this is completely false?

Ahhh! *lightbulb blinks on over 3rdalbums' head*

The reason why you're getting this error message is because the external hard disk has been used on Windows, and either Windows crashed and didn't unmount the drive properly, or somebody just yanked the cable out without doing "Safely Remove Hardware".

If the drive isn't correctly removed, it will not be unmounted cleanly, and it's not really safe to mount it on Linux. That's probably the reason it wasn't automatically appearing on the desktop in the first place.

You need to plug it into a Windows computer and then do "Safely Remove Hardware" to safely unmount the drive before using it in your Linux computer.

If you don't have a Windows machine at hand, and you desperately need to get at your disk right now, you can tell Linux to mount the drive even though it knows it's unsafe:

```
pmount -t ntfs-3g -o force /dev/sde1 simpledrive
```

---

### Post by 3rdalbum on 2008-10-07
Hmm, now I'm learning something. Apparantly pmount doesn't accept options.

What if you do the following:

```
sudo mkdir /media/simpledrive
sudo mount -t ntfs-3g -o force /dev/sde1 /media/simpledrive
sudo umount /media/simpledrive
pmount -t ntfs-3g /dev/sde1 simpledrive
```

Tell us what it says each step of the way.

This is an unusual problem you're coming across, so it's taking me a little bit of trial and error to understand exactly how to solve it.

### Post by 3rdalbum on 2008-10-07
Well, all's well that ends well. I thought the first two commands would mount the drive so that only root could write to it, and as a side-effect it would clear the NTFS logfile as though it had been properly unmounted.

The drive should now mount and unmount normally from your desktop, without having to put in any commands.

If you have any more problems with the drive, send me a Private Message. Otherwise, you can use the Thread Tools link at the top of the page to mark the thread as "solved".

---

### Post by jmurrayil on 2008-10-07
I just did a re-install of Ubuntu 8.04 (Ultimate Edition) and could not get the external USB drives to mount.  Had to (1) install pmount 

sudo apt-get install pmount

and (2) edit fstab to remove an incorrect setting for sdb1, which was set to a CD Drive.  I simply commented out the bad line.

sudo gedit /etc/fstab

and add the "#" as first character in the incorrect line.

#/dev/sdb1       /media/cdrom0   udf,iso9660 user,noauto,exec,utf8 0       0

and all is well.  My systems assigns the first ext USB drive to sdb1 and the fstab assignment of a CDrom to sdb1 is the problem.

Jim

---
